Hmm, not sure what behavior assigning False to has_text_frame would have :) A little more noodling required there I expect.

I'm inclined to believe we need three analysis pages, or maybe two slightly different ones. Here's what I'm thinking. Title is really its own thing; and it's pretty complex all on its own. So I think it deserves a page of its own. Then these two pages can just describe down to getting a Title object reference. Since that mechanism would be so similar between the two, maybe those could be combined into a single page, but I wouldn't force it if it doesn't come naturally.

Title is going to have a lot of bits to it. It has the rich/strRef distinction, it can have a border I believe, it certainly has rich text (although that's just wiring up to TextFrame I expect), and I'm pretty sure it has color, like background color (I'm not sure if that can be wired into something completely already existing or not, but certain partially).

Not all that has to be implemented, of course, you just take it as far as you want. But in the analysis we have to see a path to how we get there so we don't create a dead end and later need to take an API change.

Hey All Thanks for bringing this to my attention. The server enforces HTTPS, meaning the API token in the URL is encrypted regardless. Therefore the security issue is if a user makes an error and accidentally exposes their token by publicly posting a URL. Users have done token resets because they've exposed their token in the Headers as well, so I'm not certain removing the token GET parameter would reduce human error. I think it's something all developers can relate to (accidentally committing a key to a repo). I am not too worried about this user error given the only data right now that would be exposed is market data, which presents no PII risk to the user. So if there is user error, it presents a business risk to Tiingo, not the user. I think we are OK assuming this risk for the convenience the token GET parameter gives people. You two are doing wonderful work - its greatly appreciated by myself and I know the community.

There's two possibilities to keep in mind:
1. The timestamp itself is actually just invalid, for some reason.
2. There's some other bug and we're not parsing the record we think we're parsing.

In the latter case, an exception *should* be raised, because I don't really care about whatever's in the record. But in the former case, I still care about the rest of the record. However, due to the way the APIs are setup, the first place a client could handle the ValueError is in a call to evtx_record_xml_view, which means you lose the entire record.

Adding the wrapper here seemed like a cheap way to keep the invalid timestamp isolated without disrupting further processing. On a second glance, if the invalid filetime originates from a FiletimeTypeNode, then you'd still get an AttributeError when string() is called.

the tests fail because the tests are written for py2 semantics: `unicode()` will try `__unicode__`, and if it doesn't exist (which it often doesn't), will fall back to calling `__str__` and decoding the result with the default encoding

this is no longer the case in py3, where the more commonly-implemented `__str__` is now the text type and the workaround isn't necessary.  so your classes that implement `__bytes__` but not `__str__` stringify to the default representation, as they should.  that's why i skipped these tests on py3.

The COMMA_LIST refers to how we combine serializations. If we combined them as an actual list, it would break deserialization. It's up to the user to define the parameters to deserialize into a list if that's what they want. If we build in the list serialization, it would be very similar to re-implementing list parameters, which you were big on removing.

What we could do instead is provide a wrapper to translate between lists and commas. So instead of  `IntParameter(batch_method=COMMA_LIST)` you would do `batched_list(IntParameter())`. That way we don't need to build any special-case code in the Parameter class just for COMMA_LIST.

I am not a fan of adding the request_context to the ``make_request`` signature because we would have to modify all of the other methods of the ``Endpoint`` class. What if we did the following:

1) Keep the signature the same for all of the ``endpoint`` module
2) Introduce the idea of passing a context to ``prepare_request_dict``: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/awsrequest.py#L263
3) Add the context to AWSRequest object from the request dict when we create it: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/awsrequest.py#L294.

I also found another hiccup which should have been obvious to me, but became apparent when running the test you suggested.  Once the chunk is uploaded, the actual data in the chunk is deleted.  This obviously makes sense because otherwise by the end of the upload you'd have all the data you uploaded present in memory!  So rather than simply push the chunks to be retried into the upload queue, we should push them into the read queue so that the data is reloaded, optionally compressed, and then uploaded.  This actually simplifies the code I think.  

Anyway, with these changes, I then ran the test you suggested:
I uploaded a 72 MB file in 5 MB chunks and had each chunk pretend to fail 50% of the time.  I allowed up to 30 retry attempts (only needed 3-4 of course).  I then uploaded the same file using the current version of ua.  I downloaded the two files and ran md5sum to verify that they have the same hash.  They do.  

I ran this test with ua both with and without a compression step.  Both checked out good.

Personally I prefer to keep coverage configuration in .coveragerc, but my reasons are pretty trivial. First, `coveralls` passes `".coveragerc"` as the 'config_file' option to `coverage`, as perhaps other tools might. In the long-long ago, `coverage` would only look in that passed file for its config, so it was just generally simpler to keep it there. Second, I find it easier to cargo-cult around a .coveragerc file from project to project---it's just a 'cp' if the file specifies `source = src/`---than it is a 'setup.cfg' if the destination project already has a 'setup.cfg', which is likely.

Okay, so first thing is we don't want to use len() here, because that couples this test to the __len__() implementation, which already has its own test. The good news is we don't need it. So that line goes.

Instead, we can move the expected_xml line up there. Here's that line, then I'll explain why it works:

```python
assert tab_stops._element.xml == expected_xml
```

So the secret sauce here is that built into BaseOxmlElement is a property called `.xml` which returns the XML for that element. It's actually very handy for just taking a look at what an element's subtree looks like using a print() statement, but its core use is for just this, comparing against an expected XML snippet to make sure an operation produced the right XML. TabStops._element is the w:pPr element, so this should work just fine.

A fine point is I would use 'position' instead of 'pos'. Since .add_tab(position) is an API call, the parameter name is ours to define, and I'm thinking 'position' for that job; no reason to abbreviate and the full word is less ambiguous for an end-user learning the library. The name of the attribute `w:pos`, is *not* ours to define, and by convention we use a variable of that name to mean that attribute. So keeping them distinct makes clear to the reader we're not talking about an XML attribute here, just a float that says where we want the tab to be placed.

I would also avoid the 'new_' prefix since there's no other position to distinguish it from. It's generally good form if the main call in the test looks just like the API documentation, so in this case:

```python
tab_stop = tab_stops.add_tab_stop(position)
```

Which brings me to the method name. I think we want .add_tab_stop() here rather than add tab.

I do agree that `wait` and `show` should use the same mechanisms, because in probably 99% of cases they likely are using the exact same getters. I would rather advocate for this scheme in lieu of the flag:
```
show_command(…)
custom_show_command(…)
wait_command(…)
custom_wait_command(…)
generic_wait_command(…) # deprecate and phase out
generic_update_command(…) # remains unaltered
```

The main difference is that show and wait only rely on a single callable, so it is appropriate to say whether that kwarg bundle comes from the normal `command_type` bundle or the `custom_command_type` bundle since we do that for 'regular' commands. 

However, generic update weaves together 2-3 independent callables to accomplish something. There should only be one default for this, and that default, which fits 90% of the cases is that the setter and getter come from `command_type` and the custom piece comes from `custom_command_type`. If you deviate from that pattern, you have to get specific. I'd bet at least half of the usages of generic update that use custom getters and setters could be rewritten to not use them at all. There are a small number of cases where Swagger naming issues necessitate using the name kwarg, but the default still holds for the types.

`NotImplemented` is a special singleton value in Python that indicates that the comparison operator does not know how to compare itself to the given object. It is **not** equivalent to the `NotImplemented` exception.

In Python, comparison operators work in series. For example, say you want to compare objects `a` and `b`, so you do `a == b`. However, `a` doesn't know how to compare itself to `b`, so it returns `NotImplemented`. Python then, dynamically, runs `b == a`. If `b` knows how to compare itself to `a`, then the result of that comparison is used. If `b`, like `a`, doesn't know how to compare itself to `a`, Python will default to a raw object identity comparison, defaulting to a memory address comparison of the two objects.

Note the truth of `a == b` does not necessarily imply that `a != b` is false. Additionally, the truth of `a == b` does not imply that `b == a` is true. These comparison operators can be defined uniquely for every object to evaluate however you see fit.

For more information, see the docs on the [NotImplemented singleton](https://docs.python.org/2/library/constants.html#NotImplemented) and [comparison operators](https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html#object.__eq__).

Doesn't this also allow `bytes` access in Python 3? I am fine with dual bytes/unicode indexing in Python 2 since they coerce implicitly. However, in Python 3 (where they don't, i.e. `b'foo' != u'foo'`), dual indexing can cause confusion and would be inconsistent with Python 3 semantics.

Consider this situation in Python 3:

```python
sf = tc.SFrame({'foo': [{'bar': 100}]})
sf['foo'][0]  # returns Python dictionary
sf['foo'][0]['bar']  # Returns 100

sf[b'foo']  # This would work if we allow bytes indexing
sf[0][b'foo']  # We could allow this too if extended to SArray
sf[0][b'foo'][b'bar']  # This will never work, since we can't control the semantics of native Python types
```
I think it's better to let SFrame/SArray follow Python 3 semantics and not mix.

Style: avoid setting mutable objects as default arguments, by doing `default_widths=[]` the list is scoped outside of the method and can be accidentally mutated. Ie:

```python
>>> def foo(bar=[]):
...   bar.append(1)
...   print bar
... 
>>> foo()
[1]
>>> foo()
[1, 1]
>>> foo()
[1, 1, 1]
>>> foo()
[1, 1, 1, 1]
>>> foo()
[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]
>>> 
```

So just do `=None` instead and handle the default inside the method.
